New Site:
A friend who hiked this trail showed me where this was. It’s in Mitchell Canyon at Mt Diablo State Park. From main trail head hike about a mile to Red Road (White Canyon). Turn off. Continue another mile and a half. To trail to park boundary. Small stand of Gray pine on both sides of trail with dry seasonal creek with milkweed can be found. There were monarchs that would stay a a couple three weeks around September. Never knew it was that significant, so I’d only visited a few times over the years. We’re taking 20-30 years ago approximate. I think it was Rich Kelson, entomologist and leader of annual butterfly counts. He lives close by there in Clayton. Still works at Six Flags as the Butterfly World
 entomologist.
This appears to be primarily a nectaring site, but does support a few small transitional clusters. Reported by property owner

Coyote Point is a popular San Mateo County Park with mostly open access. However, there are a few areas that have restricted access, such as a shooting range facility and ongoing construction areas. In addition, there is a natural history museum with limited access in areas due to sensitive wildlife exhibits. All these areas are currently fenced, gated, and identified with signage. Each of these areas can be observed from a distance from outside the fencing.

Immediately to the south of Coyote Point Park, is the Poplar Creek Golf course. The Eucalyptus and Monterey Pine trees on this course may have some limited habitat potential for Monarch overwintering.

Coyote Point can be easily accessed from Highway 101 at the Peninsula Avenue exit in Burlingame.

I accessed the Sweetwood site via the Dunes Beach park entrance and parked in the parking area closest to Frenchman's Creek. The grove of trees at this site is open access on the west and north sides of Frenchman's Creek, but is posted as private property on the south side of the creek. The property appears to be owned by the Seahorse Ranch Horse riding ranch property. I did not inquire about access there. Riparian habitat in immediate proximity to creek is steep and brushy/overgrown. Poison Oak was observed there, with a nice habitat zone of willows, alders, and also, Eucalyptus towards the north side of the creek.

Site is located just east of Highway 1, a couple miles south of the Half Moon Bay harbor, along Frenchman's Creek. It is basically the continuation of an old planting of Eucalyptus, Cypress and Pine trees along the creek above the Sweetwater overwintering site. Public access is limited to a small city park and play area along the central north side of the creek. Access along the south side of the creek along Frenchman's Creek Road is posted private property with no access allowed. Public access on the north side is limited to about 100 yards at the city park only. The remainder of the north side is also private property (homes and gated private property access).
See notes from 11/29 visit

New Site:
The private estate at 4100 Calle Isabella in the Cotton Point homeowners area, San Clemente. Approx 200-300 overnighting monarchs were seen and photographed here on and around 24 Oct 2021, but left and were mostly not seen during the Thanksgiving count period of that year. 
Some monarchs, perhaps 100, were seen here up to a few days ago. They overnight on a large stand (30-40 trees) of mature Monterey Cypress (Cupressus macrocarpa). Primarily on two trees about 20m due NW of the NW most corner of the historical home (Casa). 
The estate is private, not publicly accessible and owned by Mr. Gavin Herbert, Sr., a friend of mine.

Access to the summit is open from the parking area at the end of Taft Street or the stairs near the intersection of Hillside Ave and Taft Street. Parts of the western slope of the park are private property that is designated as open space by Albany zoning rules and by traditional rights of way. But some access restrictions are in place, as evidenced by barricades at park entrances at the end of Hillside Avenue, the end of Gateview Avenue, and along Pierce Street. The restrictions are in place on account of a fire that occurred last summer, causing trees to be damaged and causing city officials and Albany Fire to be concerned about dangers to park visitors posed by risk of falling tree limbs. For information about safe routes and routes to avoid, email contactmgold@gmail.com.

New Site:
86 and 276 Lily Way, La Selva Beach, Watsonville, CA. 2 Private homes on private Dr. 
long row of old Blue Gum Eucalyptus along long (~.2 mile) N-South private gated driveway. Met owner of 276 on Open Studios Art Tour. She gave permission to survey the site, which I did on 11/23 and 11/29/22. Observed 23 monarchs sunning and flying between 9:40 and 11:00am. No clusters observed. Sunny, cool both days. Estimate ~40+ monarchs present, mostly sunning and flying. Found 1 small dead monarch in owners garden.
Access Note:
Must get permission of both owners to access the monarch trees. Diana Magor has access with notice. 
Lily Way is a private road at the end of Zils Rd off San Andreas Rd in La Selva Beach next to agricultural land to the South and Manresa State Beach to the North. 86 and 276 Lily Way are essentially adjacent to Manresa State Beach, where there is a reported Overwintering site. However, I surveyed that site in 2021 and found no monarchs there.
86 Lily Way is not gated, but 276 is. Both properties are marked. 
All the monarchs I have observed there are on the South side of the trees facing the 2 houses and adjacent fields. I did find a dead monarch on the driveway and 1 in the garden.

There are several groves of mature Eucalyptus that can be viewed from the neighborhood streets.  The streets are private drives and the groves are in yards of private property.  I parked at the intersection of Via Escondido and Sea Vista Drive near the tennis courts.  I placed a paper sign in my car window stating that I was conducting a monarch count for one hour.  There are a lot of neighbors walking along the streets.  All were very friendly and assisted me by pointing to groves.  One older gentleman owned an open field near a large grove and he walked with me through the field to get a closer look.  There is a guard that sits under an umbrella beginning around 10am near the intersection where I parked.  He did waved as I drove by and did not stop me.  There is no guard gate.

Area is fenced in and has locked gates and restricted access except for an unlocked wooden gate in a church parking lot on Teloma Drive just north of Telegraph Road, Ventura. Cluster Specific Info: 150 monarchs in tree #162, cluster was extremely loose at a height of about 10-12 meters. alternatively there were many mini-clusters and many loners more or less associated with the group as a whole. however, i listed all of the above as one cluster. i counted roughly 75 from two different angles each, about 180 degrees apart, so estimated 150. the more-or-less clustered monarchs on tree #162 covered a patch spanning several square meters, in contrast to other times/places where i've observed that 150 monarchs can cluster very closely in only about 1 square foot.

(note also that elsewhere on tree #162, we also saw 6 unambiguous loners many wingspans away from the main group[s])

40 monarchs in tree #232 `dead leaf cluster' with no green leaves, only a brown ones, at about 10 meters height.

New Site:
Crescent Park, Hartnett Av & Bayview Av, Richmond, California

Observer Jan Burnham told MG and LL about the site, mentioning that she had observed some Monarch activity there on a previous day, and mentioning the presence of red eucalpytus, which we confirmed. The site is only a few blocks from site 2839, Booker T Anderson Jr (Eastshore) park. Also, the site is less than 1/2 mile in a straight line to  the monitoring area of site 2840, Richmond Field Station, though that straight line passes over a boulevard, train track, and freeway.

This site is within the golf course. Parking is in a lot outside the course, and there is a walk through gate. You can enter the gate and walk the perimeter of the course around to the eucalyptus trees which are on the far side (north) of the course along El Campo Rd and nearer the drive in entrance where you entered to park in CR. Last year I (DB) had no issues with golfers. This year, a groundskeeper told CH she’d have to come in the morning when no golfers were there. However we did find golfers present and for the most part they were excited about the monarchs. One man was grumpy about us being there. We stayed off the course until all golfers played through, which wasn’t many since it was supposed to be closed.

New Site: Grove of primarily Blue Gum Eucalyptus trees located in a swale/drainage in the center of Space Launch Complex 6 (SLC-6) on Vandenberg Space Force Base.  Saw the grove on an aerial photo (Google Earth) and decided it looked potentially suitable so went to check it out.  Main portion of grove is accessible from North Access Rd (N Rd on Google Maps though this is incorrect).  Small separate grove to the south accessible  only via locked gate on Lunar Rd which requires a special key.  Unclear whether the two groves should be lumped or considered separate.
Access Note: Located on VSFB.  Accessible via North Access Road, which is off of Coast Road, north of the intersection of Lunar & Coast Rd.  The road is unmarked but paved, and proceeds to a locked gate in front of building 521, on the north side of the main SLC-6 area.  There is a parking lot on the north side of North Access Road before the locked gate.  Park here, walk south across the road, and follow the smaller (west) powerline corridor down into the trees.  The corridor was trimmed/cut many years ago and stumps are evident.  One cluster site is in this area, in smaller trees that have resprouted.  A second cluster location is to the SW on the other side of large trees.  Other cluster locations are to the east, and one is in the SE part of the grove.

New Site: Eucalyptus grove next to Space Launch Complex 6 (SLC-6) on Vandenberg Space Force Base.  I also submitted a count from 10/30/23 with more detailed location information, and a (short) habitat assessment form.
Access Note: Located on VSFB next to SLC-6.

For the remaining private property, I have permission from the landowners to go on their property.  In sum, I have effectively "open" access although it is not an area which is completely open to the public.  I have been formally surveying/monitoring this site for 15+ years.

I parked at the intersection of Via Escondido and Sea Vista Dr. by the tennis club and walked the streets (walkers along the street said it wasn't a problem).  All the potential host trees are on private property or in a ravine that can only be accessed via private property.  There is some viewing ability from the roads.

New Site:
Monarchs are in cypress trees in riparian habitat around Young Creek. 
Park at Ragged Point Inn, in the farthest north part of the lot, by the gas station.  Walk a little north on the Highway 1 shoulder (it is wide, and safe) until you see the gate on the other side of the Highway.  Cross the highway and walk around the gate, then follow the trail to the left (under large fallen tree) down into the riparian.  Before you get to the wooden foot bridge there is an open area around the creek; clusters were in cypress branches hanging over the drainage.
This site is probably private property, but there is open access to the south of the gate, via a trail that is directly across from the gas station parking lot.  You can also enter via that open trail, then turn left and walk north for ~200 feet through the cypress grove and down to the creek.

New Site:
The site where the monarchs were observed is the Glen Cove Waterfront Park (Whitesides Dr, Vallejo, CA 94591). They occupy a small eucalyptus grove on a slope between the Bay Area Ridge Trail and a street called South Regatta Drive.

I learned of the site after seeing a few posters at the park's entrance from Solano RCD and the Monarch Milkweed Project. The posters informed visitors that monarchs were suspected to migrate in this area.

I've scouted this park several times since late October of this year. I've seen them flying around or sunning on the plants within the park (mostly on or near the slope where the grove was), but most of the monarchs I observed were coming from the South Regatta side.
Access Note: The park itself is public and remains open from sunrise to sunset, but the grove within the area is barricaded by logs. On the South Regatta side, it is not barricaded. However, the grove is on a slope that is steep and may pose as a safety hazard. South Regatta Drive is also the highest point of this slope.

New Site: Arcadia Ecohome abuts Camino Grove Elementary School & Camino Park and is one block from Santa Ana Wash in the historic Camino Grove neighborhood of Arcadia, a foothill community of the Angeles National Forest / San Gabriel Mountains. In addition to trees in the school & park, along the wash and around the neighborhood, the Ecohome is a homestead with a pocket forest made of 550+ trees, shrubs & vines and uncountable ground covers in diversified species. The Ecohome is a Registered Pollinator Habitat of the Xerces Society’s Bring Back the Pollinators project, a Certified Wildlife Habitat, a Certified Pollinator Habitat, a Certified Bee Friendly Farm, and a Certified-Registered Monarch Waystation. It is also a participatory site serving as a  observational lab/station for a number of community/citizen science projects.
Access Note: Arcadia Ecohome is a private property controlled and managed by family members with no restrictions to access. It is Limited Access to other people who can gain the access upon our permission.
